messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_doc_06f8fa1cac644fe2.pdf
Equipment class: cooling_tower

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_9ae25f0a37a70b84", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cooling tower piping design", "summary": "Piping from tower sump to pump must be designed with sump level above pump casing for positive prime, and all piping pitched up to eliminate air pockets.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Cooling tower piping design", "summary": "Piping from tower sump to pump must be designed with sump level above pump casing for positive prime, and all piping pitched up to eliminate air pockets."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS
Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction
strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indi-
cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]. Use fric-
tion factors for aged or old pipes.
In cooling tower installations, ascertain that sufficient NPSH
(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow
through each cooling tower has th e same pressure drop. Install
equalizing lines of proper size between towers; otherwise, one
tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_272c0bfd054305b9", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Cooling tower piping velocity range", "summary": "Piping is normally sized to yield water velocities between 5 and 12 fps (1.5 and 3.7 m/s).", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "water velocity", "value": "5-12 fps", "unit": "fps", "title": "Cooling tower piping velocity range", "summary": "Piping is normally sized to yield water velocities between 5 and 12 fps (1.5 and 3.7 m/s)."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS

cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]. Use fric-
tion factors for aged or old pipes.

(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow
through each cooling tower has th e same pressure drop. Install
equalizing lines of proper size between towers; otherwise, one

pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction

cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield

(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow
through each cooling tower has th e same pressure drop. Install
equalizing lines of proper size between towers; otherwise, one
tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_f62143b985c3d935", "knowledge_type": "maintenance_procedure", "title": "Winterizing cooling tower piping", "summary": "Winterize tower piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off; isolation valves must be in heated space.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Winterizing cooling tower piping", "summary": "Winterize tower piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off; isolation valves must be in heated space."}, "evidence": "Winterize the tower
piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off.
In the latter case, the isolation va lves must be located in a heated
space. Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single
cell can be serviced while the others are active.
5.4.6 Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative condensers are used to  reject heat to the outdoor
air by means of the evaporation of water. Water is sprayed on the
condenser coils, and the resultant heat of vaporization, combined
with any sensible heat effect, co ols the liquid inside the coils. The
residual spray water is usually recirculated. An evaporative con-
denser uses much less makeup water than a cooling tower in a con-
ventional water-cooled condenser arrangement (approximately 1.6
to 2.0 gph/ton [0.5 to 0.6 mL/s pe r kW] by evaporation plus 0.5 to
0.6 gph/ton [0.15 to 0.18 mL/s pe r kW] for bleed water). Evapora-
tive condensers are used primarily  as refrigeration system condens-
ers.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_900104c7874c10e5", "knowledge_type": "maintenance_procedure", "title": "Cooling tower cell isolation for service", "summary": "Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single cell can be serviced while others are active.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Cooling tower cell isolation for service", "summary": "Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single cell can be serviced while others are active."}, "evidence": "Winterize the tower
piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off.
In the latter case, the isolation va lves must be located in a heated
space. Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single
cell can be serviced while the others are active.
5.4.6 Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative condensers are used to  reject heat to the outdoor
air by means of the evaporation of water. Water is sprayed on the
condenser coils, and the resultant heat of vaporization, combined
with any sensible heat effect, co ols the liquid inside the coils. The
residual spray water is usually recirculated. An evaporative con-
denser uses much less makeup water than a cooling tower in a con-
ventional water-cooled condenser arrangement (approximately 1.6
to 2.0 gph/ton [0.5 to 0.6 mL/s pe r kW] by evaporation plus 0.5 to
0.6 gph/ton [0.15 to 0.18 mL/s pe r kW] for bleed water). Evapora-
tive condensers are used primarily  as refrigeration system condens-
ers.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_07a8e1c5a8ea3823", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Evaporative condenser water consumption", "summary": "Evaporative condenser uses approximately 1.6 to 2.0 gph/ton by evaporation plus 0.5 to 0.6 gph/ton for bleed water.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "water consumption", "value": "1.6-2.0 gph/ton evaporation + 0.5-0.6 gph/ton bleed", "unit": "gph/ton", "title": "Evaporative condenser water consumption", "summary": "Evaporative condenser uses approximately 1.6 to 2.0 gph/ton by evaporation plus 0.5 to 0.6 gph/ton for bleed water."}, "evidence": "This may limit system selection options
where the resulting appearance is not acceptable to the architect
or client.
Cooling towers, usually located on  the roof, affect the physical
appearance of a building. Other ro oftop equipment, such as pack-
aged air conditioners, air-cooled condensers, and air intake and
exhaust hoods/fans, may have a similar effect. Coordination of
intake/exhaust locations and louvers will be required for ventilation
heat recovery systems. Attractive architectural enclosures can
reduce the aesthetic disadvantage of much rooftop equipment.
Under certain conditions, such equi pment can be placed at ground
level and camouflaged by lands caping. Locating condenser/com-
pressor units for buildings with multiple split systems can be espe-.", "source_pages": [3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_2fa7fb49b09811cd", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Evaporative condenser location", "summary": "Evaporative condensers should be located as close as possible to refrigeration machinery to keep refrigerant lines short.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Evaporative condenser location", "summary": "Evaporative condensers should be located as close as possible to refrigeration machinery to keep refrigerant lines short."}, "evidence": "In such applications, they should be located as close as possible
to the refrigeration machinery to keep the refrigerant lines short. In
the winter, the water can often be shut off with the dry coil provid-
ing adequate heat rejection. Evaporative condensers are also used to
cool recirculating water in closed-loop water-source heat pump sys-
tems (see Chapter 7).
Figure 5-8 shows two evaporative condenser types using city,
well, or river makeup water. Piping should be sized in accordance
with the principles outlined in Section 5.4.5 with flow velocities of
5 to 10 fps [1.5 to 3.0 m/s]. Where city water is used, a pump is
usually not required since the water arrives under pressure. For
well or river water, pumps may be necessary, in which case the.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_d2ed00ba495ada00", "knowledge_type": "operational_sequence", "title": "Winter operation of evaporative condenser", "summary": "In winter, water can often be shut off with the dry coil providing adequate heat rejection.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Winter operation of evaporative condenser", "summary": "In winter, water can often be shut off with the dry coil providing adequate heat rejection."}, "evidence": "10⏐ THE DESIGN  PROCESS
be benchmarked with a criterion that requires compliance with
ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1,  Energy Standard for Build-
ings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings .
Design validation  involves the use of a wide range of esti-
mates, calculations, simulations, and related techniques to confirm
that a chosen design option will in fact meet the appropriate design
criteria. Design validation is esse ntial to successful design; other-
wise there is no connection betwee n design intent and design deci-
sions. Pre- and  post-occupancy validations  are also important to
ensure that the construction process and ensuing operational proce-
dures have delivered design intent. Such validations are a key
aspect of building commissioning.
2.2 DESIGN VERSUS ANALYSIS
Anyone who has taken a course in mathematics or any of the
physical sciences is familiar with the process of analysis.", "source_pages": [2, 3, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_0dbb1f5a67cd7309", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Evaporative condenser piping velocity range", "summary": "Piping should be sized with flow velocities of 5 to 10 fps (1.5 to 3.0 m/s).", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "water velocity", "value": "5-10 fps", "unit": "fps", "title": "Evaporative condenser piping velocity range", "summary": "Piping should be sized with flow velocities of 5 to 10 fps (1.5 to 3.0 m/s)."}, "evidence": "In such applications, they should be located as close as possible
to the refrigeration machinery to keep the refrigerant lines short. In
the winter, the water can often be shut off with the dry coil provid-
ing adequate heat rejection. Evaporative condensers are also used to
cool recirculating water in closed-loop water-source heat pump sys-
tems (see Chapter 7).
Figure 5-8 shows two evaporative condenser types using city,
well, or river makeup water. Piping should be sized in accordance

5 to 10 fps [1.5 to 3.0 m/s]. Where city water is used, a pump is
usually not required since the water arrives under pressure. For
well or river water, pumps may be necessary, in which case the.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_180eda0e2f0d68ea", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Suction strainer gauges", "summary": "Suction strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indicate when cleaning is required.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Suction strainer gauges", "summary": "Suction strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indicate when cleaning is required."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS
Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction
strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indi-
cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]. Use fric-
tion factors for aged or old pipes.

(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow

tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_83b2665d63fd5d9e", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Use aged pipe friction factors", "summary": "Use friction factors for aged or old pipes when sizing piping.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Use aged pipe friction factors", "summary": "Use friction factors for aged or old pipes when sizing piping."}, "evidence": "This ma nual focuses upon applications.
The manual has two main parts: (1) a narrative description of
design procedures and criteria orga nized into ten chapters and (2) six
appendices with illustrative exam ples presented in greater detail.
The user/reader should be familiar with the general concepts of
HV AC&R equipment and possess or have access to the four-volume
ASHRAE  Handbook series and appropriate ASHRAE special publi-
cations to obtain grounding in the fundamentals of HV AC&R sys-
tem design. Information contained in the Handbooks and in special
publications is referenced—but not generally repeated—herein.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_f7db91a259161ff2", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "NPSH for condenser-water pump", "summary": "Ascertain that sufficient NPSH for the condenser-water pump is provided.", "structured_payload": {"title": "NPSH for condenser-water pump", "summary": "Ascertain that sufficient NPSH for the condenser-water pump is provided."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS

pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction

cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]. Use fric-
tion factors for aged or old pipes.

(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow
through each cooling tower has th e same pressure drop. Install
equalizing lines of proper size between towers; otherwise, one
tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_bd0ab1063dd572e4", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Equal pressure drop across cooling towers", "summary": "Design connections so that flow through each cooling tower has the same pressure drop.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Equal pressure drop across cooling towers", "summary": "Design connections so that flow through each cooling tower has the same pressure drop."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS

tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_28f144fb20b2aae5", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Evaporative condenser use in closed-loop heat pump systems", "summary": "Evaporative condensers are also used to cool recirculating water in closed-loop water-source heat pump systems.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Evaporative condenser use in closed-loop heat pump systems", "summary": "Evaporative condensers are also used to cool recirculating water in closed-loop water-source heat pump systems."}, "evidence": "11.1 PURPOSE OF THIS MANUAL
This manual was prepared to assist entry-level engineers in the
design of air-conditioning systems. It is also usable—in conjunc-
tion with fundamental HV AC&R resource materials—as a senior-
or graduate-level text for a university course in HV AC system
design. This manual was intended to fill the void between theory
and practice, to bridge the gap be tween real-world design practices
and the theoretical knowledge acquir ed in the typical college course
or textbook. Courses and texts usually concentrate on theoretical
calculations and analytical procedures or they focus upon the
design of components.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_563d1f55270550a8", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "City water supply for evaporative condenser", "summary": "Where city water is used, a pump is usually not required since the water arrives under pressure.", "structured_payload": {"title": "City water supply for evaporative condenser", "summary": "Where city water is used, a pump is usually not required since the water arrives under pressure."}, "evidence": "In such applications, they should be located as close as possible
to the refrigeration machinery to keep the refrigerant lines short. In
the winter, the water can often be shut off with the dry coil provid-
ing adequate heat rejection. Evaporative condensers are also used to
cool recirculating water in closed-loop water-source heat pump sys-
tems (see Chapter 7).
Figure 5-8 shows two evaporative condenser types using city,
well, or river makeup water. Piping should be sized in accordance
with the principles outlined in Section 5.4.5 with flow velocities of
5 to 10 fps [1.5 to 3.0 m/s]. Where city water is used, a pump is
usually not required since the water arrives under pressure. For
well or river water, pumps may be necessary, in which case the.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}]}
